title: Cold-start race can spawn multiple daemons concurrently
reasoning: `ensureDaemon()` attempts to create a lock directory but ignores failure and spawns anyway. Two CLI processes starting from a cold state will both see `ping()` fail and both launch a daemon. Each daemon then unconditionally unlinks and binds the same socket path, so one process can steal the socket path from the other and leave an orphaned daemon behind. The lock path currently does not serialize startup at all.
reproduction: From a clean runtime directory, launch two CLI commands that autostart the daemon at the same time and inspect the resulting daemon processes/socket behavior.
recommendation: Make startup single-writer: if lock acquisition fails, wait for the existing starter to finish instead of spawning. Also treat socket bind failure or unexpected socket replacement as a startup error rather than silently racing.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: There is no parallel startup test and no assertion that a cold start yields exactly one daemon process.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add an integration test that fires two concurrent requests from a clean state and asserts only one daemon instance is created and both requests succeed against the same socket.
minimumFixScope: src/ipc/client.ts startup locking and daemon single-instance handling in src/ipc/server.ts

history.reasoning: The original client evidence no longer exists in the reported form. In current src/ipc/client.ts, ensureDaemon() calls acquireStartupLock(), only spawns when mkdirSync(lockPath()) succeeds, re-pings after acquiring the lock, and when lock acquisition fails it waits for the existing starter instead of spawning. The built dist/cli.mjs contains the same logic, so the shipped CLI is also updated. The server still unconditionally unlinks and listens on the socket path in src/ipc/server.ts and dist/daemon-main.mjs, but the original cold-start race was the client spawning multiple daemons after ignoring lock acquisition failure; current client code serializes that path. Existing tests pass, including the IPC stale-socket recovery test and full suite. I could not run an ad hoc two-process race repro in this read-only sandbox because creating an isolated /tmp runtime failed with EPERM, so this conclusion is based on current source/dist control flow plus passing tests rather than a live race stress test.
commands: tmp=$(mktemp -d /tmp/glimpse-race.XXXXXX); env TMPDIR="$tmp" bun -e 'import { request } from "./src/ipc/client.ts"; await request("ping"); console.log("one done")' & p1=$!; env TMPDIR="$tmp" bun -e 'import { request } from "./src/ipc/client.ts"; await request("ping"); console.log("two done")' & p2=$!; wait $p1 $p2; state="$tmp/glimpse-cli-$(id -u)/daemon.json"; echo STATE:$state; cat "$state"; pid=$(node -e 'const fs=require("fs"); const p=process.argv[1]; if (fs.existsSync(p)) process.stdout.write(String(JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(p,"utf8")).pid));' "$state"); echo; echo PID:$pid; pgrep -fl "daemon-main.ts|daemon-main.mjs" || true; [ -n "$pid" ] && kill "$pid" 2>/dev/null || true; rm -rf "$tmp"
